request: Let users edit the reported totals and export the figures for the finance team.

problem.value: Likely: operational totals sometimes need correction/adjustment before finance can rely on them, and finance needs a formatted export instead of manual copy-paste.
problem.evidence: "edit the reported totals" + "export ... for the finance team" — no stated pain point.

current_process.evidence: Not mentioned — is this today done via spreadsheet exports, manual journal entries, or not at all?

actors.value: "Users" edit totals; "finance team" consumes the export — but which role edits (contributor? controller?) and who approves/closes is unstated.
actors.evidence: "let users edit" / "export ... for the finance team"

business_objects.value: Reporting Period, Line Item/Entry, Account, Cost Center — the 'reported totals' are presumably aggregates of these.
business_objects.evidence: Domain default per financial-reporting context; not named explicitly by client.

business_rules.value: Unknown whether an 'edit' should be an auditable adjustment entry or a direct override of the derived total.
business_rules.evidence: "edit the reported totals" is exactly the ambiguous phrase the context flags as a trap.

workflow.value: No mention of period state (open/in-review/closed) or whether edits are blocked/rerouted once closed.

integrations.value: Export destination/format/consumer unknown (accounting system? spreadsheet? bank file?).
integrations.evidence: "export the figures for the finance team" — destination and format not specified.

permissions.value: Unclear who may edit vs. who may export vs. who may see the underlying figures; segregation of duties not addressed.
permissions.evidence: "users" is undifferentiated; finance-reporting context flags edit/approve/close as distinct rights.

config_vs_custom.evidence: Not addressed — is the adjustment/override mechanism and export format standard or per-client configurable?

constraints.value: No deadline or regulatory constraint named explicitly, but financial reporting domain implies audit/compliance obligations by default.
constraints.evidence: Domain context: accounting standards, auditability, segregation of duties.

edge_cases.evidence: Retroactive edits, closed-period edits, concurrent edits, incomplete source data — none discussed.

reporting.value: An audit trail of who changed a figure, when, and why is very likely required given the domain, but not requested explicitly.
reporting.evidence: Financial-reporting context: 'an audit trail on every figure that moved' is a hard requirement, not optional.

risks.evidence: Not specified — risk of a report silently not tying back to source data if overrides are unmarked.

questions.q: When a user 'edits' a total, should the system record it as a traceable adjustment entry (amount + reason + author, total still = sum of entries) or replace the derived total with an override value that may no longer tie back to the underlying entries?
questions.why: uncertainty(high, empty) × impact(high): this decides whether the report can still reconcile to its source data — the single biggest fork in build shape and audit posture.

questions.q: Can totals be edited after the reporting period is closed, or does a closed period require a restatement / next-period adjustment instead of a direct edit?
questions.why: uncertainty(high, empty) × impact(high): editing a closed period is normally forbidden/controlled — 'edit' means something different before vs after close.

questions.q: Who is allowed to edit a figure, who may export it, and who may close the period — are these meant to be different rights (segregation of duties), or the same 'users'?
questions.why: uncertainty(high, empty) × impact(high): financial data is typically role/entity-scoped, and mixing edit/approve/close in one role breaks standard audit controls.

questions.q: Where does the export go and in what format (accounting system feed, spreadsheet for finance, a filing) — and once sent, must that exact export remain retrievable/reproducible even if figures later change?
questions.why: uncertainty(high, empty) × impact(high): export is a contract with a consumer, not a file dump — format, rounding and snapshot-retention change the build significantly.

questions.q: Is a visible audit trail (who changed which figure, previous vs new value, why) an explicit requirement for this feature, e.g. for an auditor or regulator?
questions.why: uncertainty(high, empty) × impact(high, raised by domain): financial reporting almost always needs this, and retrofitting an audit trail later is expensive.

summary.objective: Allow authorized users to correct reported financial totals and provide finance with an exportable, reliable set of figures.
summary.scope: Likely includes: an edit mechanism for reported totals (adjustment entry or override — TBD), period-state awareness (open vs closed), role-based permissions for editing/exporting/closing, an export to a finance-consumable format, and an audit trail of changes.
assumptions: The 'reported totals' are derived aggregates over Reporting Period × Account × Entity, not standalone fields.
Users editing totals are distinct from the finance team consuming the export, though exact roles are unconfirmed.
An audit trail of edits is expected given the financial-reporting domain, even though not explicitly requested.
Editing is expected to be restricted or rerouted once a period is closed.
summary.blind_spot: "Edit the total" is being requested as if it were a simple field update, but in this domain it hides a fork between an auditable adjustment entry (traceable, reversible, sum still adds up) and a silent override (the report stops tying back to its own source data) — and whether that edit is even allowed once the period is closed. Building the wrong one produces a report finance and auditors can't trust.

problem.value: Finance needs a way to correct or influence reported totals and hand off clean figures to the finance team — likely to fix errors, reflect late entries, or produce a period-end deliverable.
problem.evidence: "Let users edit the reported totals and export the figures for the finance team."

actors.value: Likely a controller/analyst edits, finance manager approves/closes, finance team receives the export — but roles for 'users' are unspecified.
actors.evidence: "users" and "finance team" mentioned without role distinction

business_objects.value: Reporting Period, Line Item/Entry, Account, likely Adjustment, and an Export artifact.

business_rules.value: Unknown whether editing a total creates a traceable Adjustment entry or overrides the derived value directly — these are two different builds with very different audit consequences.
business_rules.evidence: "edit the reported totals" — request doesn't say how

workflow.value: Unclear whether edits are allowed on open periods only, or also on closed ones (which would require a restatement/reopen path).

integrations.value: Export destination/format for the finance team is unspecified — could be a downstream accounting system with a strict schema, or a simple file for humans.
integrations.evidence: "export the figures for the finance team"

permissions.value: Who may edit vs. who may approve/close is not specified — segregation of duties is a standard requirement in this domain.

config_vs_custom.value: Export format/field mapping may need to be client-specific.

constraints.value: Financial figures imply auditability, segregation-of-duties and possibly statutory retention/filing obligations.

edge_cases.value: Rounding vs. aggregation mismatch, multi-currency FX-date convention, and re-export after a figure changes are unaddressed.

reporting.value: An audit trail on every edited figure (who/when/why/previous value) is very likely required, not optional, given the domain.
reporting.evidence: impact_signal: financial figures + edit + export to finance = compliance stakes

questions.q: When a user 'edits' a total, should this be recorded as a separate, attributed Adjustment entry (traceable, reversible) or should it overwrite the derived value directly (an override that can silently drift from the underlying data)?
questions.why: uncertainty high (empty) × impact high: this is the single biggest fork in the build — adjustment and override are different data models, different audit stories, and different user flows.

questions.q: Can totals be edited once their Reporting Period is closed, or must a correction after close go through a restatement / next-period adjustment instead of touching the closed figures?
questions.why: uncertainty high × impact high: closed periods are meant to be frozen in this domain; allowing direct edits post-close is a compliance red flag and changes the whole lifecycle design.

questions.q: Who is allowed to edit a total, and is that person different from whoever approves the edit or closes the period (segregation of duties)?
questions.why: uncertainty high × impact high: financial platforms typically require edit/approve/close to be distinct rights — building it as one undifferentiated 'user' role would need rework later.

questions.q: What is the export's destination and contract — a specific accounting system with an agreed schema, a file for the finance team to read, and does each export need to be versioned/retained as a point-in-time snapshot?
questions.why: uncertainty high × impact high: 'export' as a contract with rounding, encoding and reproducibility requirements is a very different build than a one-off file dump.

questions.q: Is a full audit trail (who changed which figure, when, previous vs. new value, and why) a requirement for this feature — e.g. for an auditor or regulator to review later?
questions.why: uncertainty high × impact high (compliance signal): retrofitting an audit trail after the fact is expensive; it must be designed in from the start if required.

summary.objective: Allow authorized users to correct reported totals and hand off the resulting figures to the finance team in a usable, trustworthy export.
summary.scope: Likely includes: an edit mechanism for totals (adjustment entry vs. override — TBD), respecting the Reporting Period lifecycle (open vs. closed), role-based permissions for who may edit/approve/close, an export flow with a defined format/destination, and an audit trail of changes.
assumptions: Editing a total will be implemented as a traceable Adjustment entry rather than a silent override, pending confirmation.
Edits are only permitted on open/in-review periods; closed periods require restatement.
Edit and approve/close are distinct roles (segregation of duties).
The export target is a defined downstream system or file format, not an ad-hoc dump.
An audit trail of who-changed-what-when is required given the financial/compliance domain.
summary.blind_spot: "Edit the total" looks like a simple field update, but a reported total is a derived value — the real question is whether edits are auditable adjustment entries or raw overrides, and whether they're even allowed once the period is closed. Get this wrong and the report silently stops tying back to its own source data, which is exactly what finance/audit will catch first.

problem.value: Finance needs a way to correct/adjust figures before sending them onward, and a way to hand off numbers to the finance team in a usable format.
problem.evidence: "Let users edit the reported totals and export the figures for the finance team" — no stated root cause (error correction? period close prep? recurring handoff?).

current_process.evidence: Not mentioned — unknown whether totals are currently fixed, manually patched in Excel after export, or not editable at all today.

actors.value: Likely: operational contributor (source data), controller/analyst (edits), finance manager (approves/closes), auditor (read-only review of the edit).
actors.evidence: "users" and "finance team" named but roles/rights not distinguished — context shows these are normally separate rights.

business_objects.value: Reporting Period, Line Item/Entry, Account, Export — 'totals' and 'figures' map to these but exact dimension (period × account × entity) unspecified.
business_objects.evidence: "reported totals" and "figures" named directly, but as aggregates not raw entities.

business_rules.value: Unclear whether 'edit the total' means an auditable adjustment entry (traceable, reversible) or a raw override (replaces the derived value, breaks tie-out to source data).
business_rules.evidence: Context flags this exact ambiguity as the #1 recurring trap for this domain; request gives zero indication which is intended.

workflow.value: No mention of period status (open/in-review/closed) gating whether an edit is even allowed, or whether editing a closed period requires restatement/reopening instead.
workflow.evidence: Domain notes: a closed period's figures are frozen; 'edit' means something different before/after close.

integrations.value: Export target assumed to be an external consumer (accounting system, or a spreadsheet handed to finance) but format, field set, and whether it must reconcile to a filed export are unknown.
integrations.evidence: "export the figures for the finance team" implies a downstream consumer but not which one or in what shape.

permissions.value: Who may edit vs. approve the edit vs. export vs. merely view is undifferentiated — segregation of duties is a named domain concern.
permissions.evidence: "users" is undifferentiated; context stresses see/adjust/close are distinct rights, often scoped by entity/cost center.

config_vs_custom.value: Unknown whether adjustment rules, approval circuits, and export formats are standard or must vary per client.
config_vs_custom.evidence: Platform is configurable multi-client; context lists adjustment approval circuits and export field mappings as client-specific by default.

constraints.value: Likely regulatory pressure (audit trail, segregation of duties, statutory retention) even though not stated, since this is financial reporting for a finance team.
constraints.evidence: Domain constraints section names auditability and segregation of duties as recurring, non-optional concerns for this module.

edge_cases.value: Editing a closed period, rounding vs. aggregation mismatch, multi-currency FX date, concurrent edits — none addressed.
edge_cases.evidence: Not mentioned; all flagged as recurring traps in the context.

reporting.value: An audit trail (who changed which figure, when, old vs new value, why) is implied as a hard requirement by the domain, not optional — impact raised from default low.
reporting.evidence: impact_signals triggered: financial reporting context explicitly names auditability of changed figures as a recurring, non-negotiable need.

risks.value: Main risk: a silent override that decouples the report from its source data, or an export that doesn't reconcile after a later edit.
risks.evidence: Directly named as a domain trap: 'an override without a visible marker is how a report quietly stops tying back to the system.'

questions.q: When a user 'edits a total', should this create a traceable adjustment entry (a new line that explains the delta) or overwrite the derived value directly?
questions.why: uncertainty × impact: empty × high — these are two different builds (auditable entry vs. silent override) and the request doesn't say which; getting this wrong breaks reconciliation to source data.

questions.q: Can totals be edited after the reporting period is closed — and if so, does that mean reopening the period or posting the correction as an adjustment in the next open period?
questions.why: uncertainty × impact: empty × high — closed periods are normally frozen; 'edit' behaves completely differently before vs. after close, and reopening a closed period is usually a controlled, logged, sometimes forbidden act.

questions.q: Who is allowed to edit a total, who must approve that edit, and is that different from who can export or close the period?
questions.why: uncertainty × impact: empty × high — segregation of duties is a named domain concern (the person who adjusts shouldn't be the one who closes); undifferentiated 'users' hides this.

questions.q: What is the export's destination and contract — which system or team, what format/fields, and must a previously sent export stay retrievable even after the figures later change?
questions.why: uncertainty × impact: inferred × high — export is described as a contract with a downstream consumer, not a file dump; format mismatch is rejected downstream, and a later edit can make a prior export stale.

questions.q: Do adjustment rules, approval circuits, or export formats need to vary by client/entity/cost-center, or is one standard flow enough?
questions.why: uncertainty × impact: empty × high — platform is multi-client configurable; adjustment approval circuits and export mappings are client-specific by default, which changes the build shape significantly.

summary.objective: Allow authorized users to correct reported financial totals and hand off the resulting figures to the finance team in a usable, reliable export.
summary.scope: Likely includes: an edit mechanism for totals (adjustment entry or override — TBD), period-status-aware edit rules, an audit trail of who changed what and why, role-gated access (edit/approve/export/close as distinct rights), and an export pipeline with an agreed, versioned, reproducible format.
assumptions: Actors include a controller/analyst who edits, a finance manager who approves/closes, and an auditor with read-only access (inferred, not stated).
An audit trail on every edited figure is required, given the financial-reporting domain's inherent compliance/audit needs.
The export target is an external system or the finance team's own tooling, not yet specified.
Regulatory/segregation-of-duties constraints apply even though not explicitly mentioned by the client.
summary.blind_spot: "Edit the total" is being requested as if it were a simple field update, but a reported total is a derived value: the real design decision is whether an edit is a traceable adjustment entry or a silent override that decouples the report from its own source data — and whether editing is even allowed once the period is closed. Building the wrong one undermines the very reconciliation the export is meant to guarantee.
